title: Why AC beat DC
what: Alternating current can be stepped up to very high voltage with a transformer; Edison's direct current could not, so it could only travel a kilometre or two before fading.
why: High voltage lets power travel far with little loss, so AC could serve whole regions from one plant while DC needed a station every few blocks.
care: It's the reason a distant power plant can light your home — and why wall sockets everywhere deliver AC.

title: Why power lines run at high voltage
what: Transmission lines carry electricity at tens or hundreds of thousands of volts, then transformers step it down near your neighbourhood.
why: For a given amount of power, higher voltage means lower current, and lower current wastes far less energy as heat in the wires.
care: It's why a plant 300 km away can serve your city without melting the cables — and why those towers are so tall.

title: How transformers changed civilisation
what: A transformer uses two coils around an iron core; alternating current in one coil induces a voltage in the other, raising or lowering it with almost no loss.
why: It lets the grid use high voltage for long-distance travel and low, safe voltage in your home — the trick that made AC practical.
care: The hum of the grey cylinders on power poles is transformers quietly making your electricity safe to use.

title: Why the grid must hold a steady frequency
what: Grids run at a fixed frequency — 60 hertz in the Americas, 50 hertz in most of the world — meaning the current reverses 100 or 120 times a second.
why: Every generator must spin in lock-step; if demand outruns supply the frequency sags, and operators add power within seconds to keep it stable.
care: That invisible heartbeat keeps clocks accurate and motors running right — and if it drifts too far, the grid trips off to protect itself.

title: How almost all electricity is made
what: A generator spins magnets past coils of wire; the moving magnetic field pushes electrons along the wire, creating current.
why: Coal, gas, nuclear, hydro, and wind nearly all do the same final step — spin a generator — just using different ways to turn the shaft.
care: Whether your power comes from a dam or a reactor, the last step is the same spinning magnet Faraday discovered in 1831.

title: Most power plants just boil water
what: Coal, gas, nuclear, geothermal, and solar-thermal plants all make heat to boil water into steam, which spins a turbine connected to a generator.
why: The differences are mostly in how the heat is made; the steam turbine at the end is remarkably similar across them.
care: A nuclear reactor, for all its mystique, is fundamentally a very sophisticated way to boil water.

title: Watts versus watt-hours
what: A watt measures power — the rate of using energy right now. A watt-hour measures energy — a watt sustained for an hour.
why: A 1,000-watt heater run for one hour uses one kilowatt-hour; confusing the two is like mixing up speed with distance.
care: Your electricity bill charges for kilowatt-hours, so knowing the difference helps you see what actually costs money.

title: How nuclear fission makes heat
what: A neutron splits a uranium atom, releasing energy and more neutrons that split further atoms in a controlled chain reaction.
why: The energy appears as heat, which boils water to drive a steam turbine — no burning and no carbon dioxide.
care: A single uranium fuel pellet the size of a fingertip holds about as much energy as a tonne of coal.

title: How a reactor is kept under control
what: Control rods made of neutron-absorbing materials slide between the fuel; pushing them in soaks up neutrons and slows the reaction.
why: By adjusting the rods, operators fine-tune the chain reaction or shut it down within seconds in an emergency.
care: This constant control is what separates a power reactor from a bomb — the physics simply won't allow a nuclear explosion.

title: Why electricity is hard to store
what: Unlike water or fuel, electricity can't easily be kept in bulk; it must usually be made the instant it's used.
why: So grid operators constantly match generation to demand, second by second, rather than drawing from a big reservoir.
care: It's why batteries and pumped-hydro are such a big deal — they finally let us bank electricity for later.

title: The grid balances on a knife's edge
what: At every instant, the power flowing onto the grid must equal the power being drawn off it.
why: Operators forecast demand and dispatch plants minute by minute; a sudden mismatch shows up immediately as a frequency change.
care: This relentless balancing act, invisible to you, is what keeps the lights steady when millions switch things on at once.

title: Why the grid uses three-phase power
what: The grid sends three alternating currents, each offset by a third of a cycle, usually on three wires.
why: Three-phase delivers steady, smooth power and lets motors run efficiently without needing a separate starter.
care: It's why big transmission towers carry wires in sets of three, and why industrial machines run so smoothly.

title: Why birds on power lines don't get shocked
what: Electricity flows through a path only when there's a voltage difference; a bird touching one wire is all at the same voltage, so nothing flows through it.
why: Current needs somewhere to go; touching two wires at once, or a wire and the ground, would create that path.
care: It explains both the bird's safety and why touching a downed line while standing on the ground is so dangerous.

title: How a battery stores energy
what: A battery stores energy in chemicals; charging drives a reaction one way, and discharging lets it reverse, pushing electrons out as current.
why: In a lithium-ion cell, charging moves lithium ions to one electrode and discharging lets them flow back, releasing power.
care: Understanding this explains why batteries wear out — the chemistry slowly degrades with every charge cycle.

title: Transmission versus distribution
what: Transmission lines are the high-voltage 'motorways' carrying power long distances; distribution lines are the local 'streets' bringing it to homes at low voltage.
why: Substations with transformers connect the two, stepping voltage down in stages along the way.
care: When a storm knocks out 'the power', it's usually the local distribution lines, not the big towers, that failed.

title: Base-load versus peaking plants
what: Base-load plants run steadily around the clock; peaking plants switch on briefly to cover spikes, like a hot evening when everyone runs air conditioning.
why: Cheap-to-run but slow plants cover the steady demand, while fast, pricier ones handle short peaks.
care: This mix is why electricity can cost far more at 6 p.m. than at 3 a.m. — and why shifting use saves money.

title: How solar panels make electricity
what: Sunlight knocks electrons loose in a layered silicon cell, and the cell's built-in electric field pushes them out as direct current.
why: There are no moving parts and no fuel — just light hitting silicon — so panels are quiet and nearly maintenance-free.
care: It's why rooftop solar can make power silently on your roof for decades with almost no upkeep.

title: How wind turbines convert wind
what: Wind pushes shaped blades that spin a shaft; gearing speeds it up to turn a generator, making electricity.
why: Bigger blades sweep more air, so modern turbines are huge — some blades are longer than a football pitch.
care: A single large offshore turbine can power thousands of homes, which is why they keep getting taller.

title: What 'capacity factor' really means
what: Capacity factor is how much a plant actually produces over a year compared with running flat-out the whole time.
why: A solar farm rated at 100 megawatts may average 25%, because it makes nothing at night; a nuclear plant often exceeds 90%.
care: It's why you can't compare power sources by their headline rating alone — uptime matters as much as size.

title: When the grid uses high-voltage DC
what: For very long overhead lines and undersea cables, the grid sometimes converts AC into high-voltage direct current.
why: Over great distances, HVDC loses less energy than AC and can link grids that aren't synchronised with each other.
care: Long undersea links that import clean power between countries rely on this — DC quietly making a comeback.

title: Why spinning machines steady the grid
what: The heavy spinning rotors of conventional generators store momentum, called inertia, that briefly resists sudden frequency changes.
why: This buys operators precious seconds to respond when a plant trips off; solar and wind don't naturally provide it.
care: As grids add more renewables, engineers must replace this 'shock absorber' with batteries and clever electronics.

title: How fuses and breakers protect you
what: A fuse melts and a circuit breaker trips when too much current flows, instantly cutting the circuit.
why: Excess current means overheating wires and fire risk; breaking the circuit stops it before damage spreads.
care: It's why a tripped breaker is a feature, not a fault — it just saved your wiring from overload.

title: Electrons crawl, but power races
what: Individual electrons drift through a wire slower than a walking pace, yet the electrical effect travels near the speed of light.
why: Flipping a switch nudges the whole chain of electrons almost at once, like pushing one end of a full pipe of marbles.
care: It's why your light turns on instantly even though the electrons themselves barely move.

title: How pumped-hydro stores energy
what: When power is cheap, water is pumped uphill to a reservoir; when demand peaks, it flows back down through turbines to make electricity.
why: It's effectively a giant water battery and stores the vast majority of the world's grid-scale energy today.
care: It's the proven way grids have banked surplus power for nearly a century — long before lithium batteries.

title: Brownouts versus blackouts
what: A blackout is a total loss of power; a brownout is a deliberate voltage reduction to ease strain when demand is dangerously high.
why: Dimming the grid slightly can shed enough load to avoid a full collapse.
care: If your lights ever dim and flicker on a scorching afternoon, the grid may be protecting itself from a blackout.

title: Why wires are copper and aluminium
what: Copper conducts electricity superbly and is used in homes; long overhead lines use aluminium because it's much lighter and cheaper.
why: Lighter wire means fewer and cheaper support towers over hundreds of kilometres, even if aluminium conducts a bit less well.
care: It's a daily engineering trade-off — performance versus cost and weight — visible in the lines overhead.

title: Sizing plants in megawatts and gigawatts
what: A megawatt is a million watts; a gigawatt is a billion. A large reactor is about one gigawatt.
why: Roughly, one gigawatt can power somewhere around 750,000 average homes, depending on the country and season.
care: These units let you judge whether a new project is a token gesture or a serious chunk of a country's power.

title: What grid operators mean by 'the load'
what: The load is the total electricity being demanded by everyone on the grid at a given moment.
why: It rises and falls predictably — low overnight, peaking on hot evenings — and operators schedule plants to follow it.
care: When you hear of 'record demand' in a heat wave, that's the load testing the limits of available supply.

title: What a smart meter actually does
what: A smart meter records your electricity use in short intervals and reports it to the utility automatically.
why: It enables time-of-use pricing, faster outage detection, and feedback that helps households cut waste.
care: It can let you pay less by shifting heavy use — laundry, charging — to cheaper, cleaner hours.

title: Why some power does no work
what: Motors and transformers draw 'reactive' power that sloshes back and forth without doing useful work, described by the power factor.
why: Utilities must supply this extra current and size their equipment for it, even though it never lights a bulb.
care: It's a hidden inefficiency that big users are charged for and that engineers work hard to correct.
